为什么现在有这么多科技公司首次公开募股(IPO)？初创公司发现，这些天来，他们可以从公开市场获得比私人市场更高的估值，因为有如此多的公共投资者希望将大量资金投入科技领域。此外，未来的诱惑，美联储的仁慈，散户投资者的繁荣，等待任何体面时刻的大量独角兽公司，公司上市的新方式…。这些都是Alex Wilhelm在审查了最新上市公司和公开市场科技公司的季度数据后发现的一些原因。
各种政治和经济动荡可能会结束这场争夺战，但对初创企业世界的影响已经到来。在时事通讯深入探讨股票、SPAC、新兴产业和其他有用的创业新闻之前，请考虑一分钟。
在这股IPO热潮中，将会有另一波初创员工财富涌入邻近的现实世界，但与Facebook和Twitter IPO的日子相比，这些财富会更广泛地扩散到旧金山湾区以外的地方。其中一些员工将成为投资者，可能还会成为创始人，现在上市的初创公司将用大公司的人取代这些职位。围绕技术招聘的动态将以令人惊讶的新方式进一步重塑，所有这些都将与远程工作等其他正在发生的变化结合在一起。
今天，如果你现在正在创建一家初创公司，你现在可以自信地制定出新的方式来长期建设你的公司，这是前几代创始人几乎无法想象的。
在接下来的十年里，我们可能会看到一家初创公司上市，它首先从种子前期的滚动资金中筹集资金，吸引新的合法化众筹，从目前运营的数千家风投中挑选合适的风投，或者可能是因为这家初创公司做得很好而举债。只要它愿意，它就可以保持私有，利用过去十年来已经弄清楚的各种融资和二级市场的可能性。然后，当它准备上市时，它可以在传统选择、完美的SPAC和直接上市之间做出选择，并让股东池保持对那些在整个过程中一直与公司在一起的真正信徒的青睐。
目前的这一批IPO还证明了一些其他的东西。科技不再被定义为旧金山一些无利可图、估值很高的消费科技初创公司。它可以来自任何地方，它可以解决实际问题，它可以赚到实实在在的钱，它可以不断建设和增长-前提是你可以接受一些持续的风险。难怪如今公开市场喜欢科技。
看看Root Insurance，这是一家保险科技独角兽公司，已经帮助定义了俄亥俄州哥伦布市的创业场景。这是一次“初创公司Rorscach测试”，亚历克斯本周详细介绍了其新的IPO申请。“您可以找到喜欢的东西(提高调整后的利润率！收入增长！)，你可以找到不喜欢的东西(螺旋式亏损！边距为负值！)。非常容易。“。
看来，许多保险提供商在新冠肺炎期间看到的顺风给了鲁特一个很好的提振(疫情期间驾驶下降，导致一些保险提供商退还保费)。Root正在利用这一时刻提交申请，此时它可以显示出经济状况的大幅改善。
这很聪明。但是，这些改进的经济学在传统会计中是如何证明的呢？让我们来了解一下：
Root的收入从2018年的4330万美元飙升至2019年的2.902亿美元。2020年上半年，Root管理着2.454亿美元的收入，比2019年上半年增长了135.73。
Root的亏损也大幅上升，从2018年的净亏损6910万美元增加到2019年的2.824亿美元。随着时间的推移，这家初创公司一直设法亏损更多。最近也是如此，2020年上半年净亏损1.445亿美元，令2019年上半年9700万美元的亏损相形见绌。
本周提交的另一份申请是confirm，它为客户提供销售点积分(没有信用卡的所有花招)。对于那些今天正在研究独角兽IPO实验的未来创始人来说，它也是几十年来创新如何运作的象征。
该公司是一家雄心勃勃的独角兽公司，有着实际的目标，从连续创业者马克斯·莱夫钦(Max Levin)，他也帮助塑造了现代创业公司的概念-从多年来与人共同创立贝宝(Paypal)并进行了无数天使投资，到十年前旧金山一家无利可图、估值很高的消费科技公司Slide。除了科技、幻灯片和其他社交媒体公司之外，人们并没有广泛理解这些公司帮助开创了随后的初创公司在SaaS、电子商务、金融科技和现实世界领域应用的增长和参与技术。今天，Root and Acfirm和这个IPO时代的许多其他公司都站在了那些年的教训上。
随着越来越多的初创企业利用这些投资工具轻松进入公开市场，特殊目的收购公司肯定会提供宝贵的经验教训。下面是关于这一行动的最新情况，首先是康妮·洛伊佐斯本周从一位专家那里得到的令人不安的引述。
前投资银行家、现在经营数据网站SPACInsider的克里斯蒂·马文(Kristi Marvin)表示，与过去几年相比，她正在与更多对推出SPAC感兴趣的人进行对话，并听说他们都有能力管理这些工具-并不是所有人都有能力管理这些工具。
“你会问，‘你有没有以5亿美元或更高的价格收购过一家公司？您是否在您的目标垂直领域有操作经验？你了解相关的报告要求吗？“通常，”她说，“答案是否定的。”
这是在一位有争议的优步前高管创办SPAC的背景下进行的；康妮还研究了这一新兴的高金融领域的性别代表性。与这个世界的其他地方一样，参与其中的人几乎都是男性(亚历克斯报道，实际上，在创业融资方面也是如此)。
与此同时，凯瑟琳·舒(Catherine Shu)研究了陷入困境的电动汽车初创公司Faraday Futures是如何接近SPAC计划的，而亚历克斯则仔细研究了OpenDoor面临的挑战和机遇。
我们关于移动性和交通未来的年度会议今年在网上举行，这意味着我们有很多方便访问的会议报道可以分享给读者(以及额外的Crunch订阅者)。以下是一些关键标题，可以帮助您集中点击：
SkyFlow创始人安舒·夏尔马(Anshu Sharma)解释了SaaS公司的爆炸性增长，特别是像他的公司这样的API服务提供商。“我很惊讶，对于网络安全这样关键的东西，公司对SaaS API的开放程度如此之高。”“虽然我在Salesforce期间在SaaS工作了十多年，包括一些非常大的交易，但包括银行和医疗保健公司在内的大公司的项目范围简直超出了几年前的可能性。我们已经真正从‘为什么是SaaS’进入了‘为什么不是SaaS’时代。“。亚历克斯和卢卡斯·马特尼调查了这个爆炸性利基市场的一系列顶级投资者和创始人，你可以在Extra Crunch上阅读完整的内容。
大家好，欢迎回到TechCrunch专注于风险投资的播客The Equity(现已上Twitter！)，我们在这里解开头条背后的数字。
这周，娜塔莎正在度假，所以丹尼·李和你的卑微仆人不得不独自努力。她下周就回来了，所以我们很快就会恢复集体力量。
但是，即使招待人员耗尽，我们仍有一大堆新闻要传开。还可以开玩笑，因为丹尼正想要开怀大笑。以下是概要：
Reddit联合创始人亚历克西斯·奥哈尼安(Alexis Ohanian)的新风险基金(New Venture Fund)投资于一次性相机App：丹尼和我是判断什么是酷的人，所以我们是讨论有影响力的人和新的社交应用的完美组合。这只实际上很漂亮，奥哈尼安被纳入投资项目，即他的新基金，这一点值得注意。
Zira为其帮助管理小时工的排班服务筹集了310万美元：这是我报道的一轮，调查了Zira.ai及其产品。我们的收入？它很整洁，但在竞争激烈的市场中运营。
幕府筹集了3500万美元，帮助各品牌用自己更快、更好的网站与亚马逊竞争：这是一个类似的故事。一家整洁的公司，在一个有已证实的需求(换句话说，TAM)和竞争的领域拥有整洁的产品。
Unqork耗资2.07亿美元的C系列交易突显了企业对无代码应用程序日益增长的需求：另一轮值得一提的是Unqork交易。Unqork是一项无代码服务，帮助其他企业为他们的公司创建应用程序。它像野草一样生长，因此是值得了解的东西。
嗯，五个月前的Video Startup Making Virtual Meetings让虚拟会议更有趣，在发布前筹集了3100万美元：是的，MMHMM筹集了更多资金，我们很高兴地得知，可能会在本月推出。
Remote筹集了1300万美元，创建了一个工作场所视频平台，用于简短的、自发的对话：在Slake消息传出后，这一轮对我们来说脱颖而出。谁来搭建人们爱用的未来远程工作通信平台？
然后还有一大堆其他的事情要处理，比如FirstMark SPAC的新闻，Root即将进行的IPO，以及Airbnb即将进行的公开募股(IPO)。
那太多了。我们已经尽力了。拥抱你，下周与你聊天！
股市每周一上午7点下跌。PT和周四下午，我们会尽快发布它，所以请订阅我们的苹果播客，阴天，Spotify和所有的演员。
note_en:
Why are there so many tech IPOs right now? Startups are finding that they can get higher valuations from public markets than private ones these days, because so many public investors want to put serious money in tech. Also, the lure of the future, the benevolence of the Fed, the retail investor boom, the sheer number of unicorns that have been waiting for any decent moment to go, the new ways a company can go public… these are some of the  reasons Alex Wilhelm found after reviewing the  latest listings and quarterly data about tech in public markets.
 Various political and economic turmoils threaten to end the run, but the impact to the startup world has arrived. Consider it for a minute before the newsletter dives into stocks, SPACs, emerging industries and other useful startup news.
  From this IPO boom, there’ll be another wave of startup employee wealth flooding into adjacent real-world spaces, but spread more broadly outside of the Bay Area than the days of Facebook and Twitter IPOs. Some of those employees will become investors and maybe founders, and the now-public startups will replace those positions with big-company people. The dynamics around tech hiring will be further reshaped in surprising new ways, all combined with the other changes happening like remote work.
 Today, if you’re founding a startup now, you can now confidently chart new ways to build your company long-term that previous generations of founders could barely imagine.
 This coming decade, we might see a startup go public that raises from pre-seed rolling funds first, pulls in newly legalized crowdfunding, matches with the right VCs from among the thousands that have are operating these days — or perhaps the startup raises debt because it’s doing  that well. It could stay private as long as it wants using the various financing and secondary market possibilities that have been figured out over the last decade. Then, when it is ready to go public, it could choose between traditional options, the perfect SPAC and a direct listing, and keep the shareholder pool in favor of the true believers who have been with the company over the course of the journey.
 This current group of IPOs also demonstrates something else. Tech is no longer defined as some profitless, highly valued consumer tech startup in San Francisco. It can come from anywhere, it can solve practical problems, it can make real money, and it can keep building and growing — provided you’re okay with some ongoing risk. No wonder public markets like tech these days.
 Take a look at Root Insurance, an insurtech unicorn that has already helped define the Columbus, Ohio startup scene. It’s a “startup Rorscach test,” as  Alex details this week about its new IPO filing. “You can find things to like (improving adjusted margins! revenue growth!), and you can find things to not like (spiraling losses! negative margins!) very easily.”
  It appears that the tailwind that many insurance providers have seen during COVID-19 has provided Root with a nice boost (driving fell during the pandemic, leading some insurance providers to return premiums.) Root is taking advantage of the moment by filing when it can show sharply improved economics.
 That’s smart. But how do those improved economics bear out in traditional accounting? Let’s find out:
 Root’s revenue has skyrocketed from $43.3 million in 2018 to $290.2 million in 2019. In the first half of 2020, Root managed $245.4 million in revenue, up 135.73% from what it managed in the first half of 2019.
 Root’s losses have also shot higher, from a net loss of $69.1 million in 2018 to $282.4 million in 2019. The startup has managed to consistently lose more money over time. This was also true more recently, when its H1 2020 net loss of $144.5 million dwarfed its H1 2019 loss of $97.0 million.
 The  other filing this week is for Affirm, which provides a point-of-sale credit for customers ( without all the tricks of credit cards). It’s also a symbol of how innovation works across the decades, for those future founders who are studying the IPO experiments of unicorns today.
 The company is a high-flying unicorn with a practical purpose from serial entrepreneur Max Levchin, who has also helped shape the concept of the modern startup — from cofounding Paypal and making numerous angel investments over the years, to Slide, a profitless, highly valued consumer tech company in San Francisco a decade ago. It’s not widely understood outside of tech,  Slide and other social media companies helped pioneer the growth and engagement techniques that subsequent startups applied across SaaS, e-commerce, fintech and real-world sectors. Today, Root and Affirm and many of the other companies in this era of IPOs are standing on the lessons of those years.
   Special Purpose Acquisition Companies are sure to provide valuable lessons, as a growing group of startups use these investment vehicles to ease into public markets. Here’s the latest look at the action, starting with this  disturbing quote that Connie Loizos got from one expert this week.
 According to Kristi Marvin, a former investment banker who now runs the data site  SPACInsider, she’s having, and hearing about, conversations with a much wider range of people interested in launching SPACs than in past years — and not all of them are necessarily equipped to manage the vehicles.
 “You ask, ‘Have you ever acquired a company for $500 million or more? Do you have operating experience in the vertical that you’re targeting? Do you understand the reporting requirements involved?’ Often,” she says, “the answers are no.”
 That was in the context of a controversial former Uber executive starting a SPAC; Connie also looked at gender representation in this emerging slice of high finance. Like other parts of that world, the people involve  are almost entirely men (which is also continuing to be the case in startup funding, actually,  Alex reports).
 Meanwhile, Catherine Shu examined how troubled electric vehicle startup  Faraday Futures is approaching SPAC plans, while Alex took a closer look at  the challenges and opportunities facing Opendoor.
   Our annual conference on mobility and the future of transportation happened online this year, which means we have  lots of easily accessible conference coverage to share for readers (and for Extra Crunch subscribers). Here are a few key headlines to help you focus your clicks:
      “I am surprised at how open companies are to a SaaS API for something as critical as cybersecurity,” Skyflow founder Anshu Sharma explains about the explosion of SaaS companies, and specifically API service providers like his company. “While I have spent over a decade in SaaS including some very large deals during my time at Salesforce, the scope of the projects by large companies including banks and healthcare companies is simply beyond what was a possibility just a few years ago. We have truly moved from ‘why SaaS’ to a ‘why not SaaS’ era.” Alex and Lucas Matney surveyed a range of top investors and founders in this exploding niche, and  you can read the full thing on Extra Crunch.
                 Hello and welcome back to  Equity, TechCrunch’s venture capital-focused podcast ( now on Twitter!), where we unpack the numbers behind the headlines.
 This week  Natasha was on vacation, so  Danny and  your humble servant had to endeavor alone. She’s back next week, so we’ll be back to full strength as a collective soon enough.
 But even with a depleted hosting crew, we had a mountain of news to get through. And to joke about, as Danny was in the mood for a laugh. Here’s the rundown:
 Reddit co-founder Alexis Ohanian’s New Venture Fund Invests in Disposable Camera App: Danny and I are arbiters of what is cool, so we were the perfect pair to discuss influencers and new social applications. This one is actually neat, and Ohanian’s inclusion in the investment viz his new fund was noteworthy.
 Zira raises $3.1M for its shift-scheduling service that helps manage hourly workers: This is a round that I covered, looking into  Zira.ai and its product. Our take? It’s neat, but operates in a competitive market.
 Shogun raises $35M to help brands take on Amazon with faster and better sites of their own: This is a similar story. A neat company with a neat product in a space where there is proven demand (TAM, in other words), and competition.
 Unqork’s $207M Series C underscores growing enterprise demand for no-code apps: Another round worth mentioning is the Unqork deal. Unqork is a no-code service that helps other businesses create apps for their companies. It’s growing like a weed, and is thus something worth knowing a bit about.
 Mmhmm, Five-Month-Old Video Startup Making Virtual Meetings More Fun, Raises $31 Million Pre-Launch: Yes, mmhmm has raised more money, and, we’re excited to learn, could be launching this very month.
 Remotion raises $13M to create a workplace video platform for short, spontaneous conversations: Following the Slack news,  this round stood out to us. Who will build the remote work comms platform of the future that people like to use?
 And then there was a host of other stuff to get through, like the  FirstMark SPAC news,  Root’s impending IPO and more on  Airbnb’s impending public offering.
 That was a lot. We did our best. Hugs and chat with you next week!
 Equity drops every Monday at 7:00 a.m. PT and Thursday afternoon as fast as we can get it out, so subscribe to us on  Apple Podcasts,  Overcast,  Spotify and all the casts.

title_en: Public investors stay in love with tech, as Root and Affirm file to IPO

note:
首先，我们热烈祝贺你们度过了这一周。如果你住在美国，你刚刚经历了有史以来最疯狂的新闻周之一。自从上周五总统宣布他对新冠肺炎持积极态度以来，头条新闻和近乎恐慌一直是我们的命运。我们都很累了。你只要活下来就能加分。
第二，这个周末我想带给你一些振奋人心的东西，这是你应得的。遗憾的是，这不是我们要谈的。
周五，该交易所涵盖了有关第三季度女性创始人风险投资业绩的新数据。该数据集是以美国为重点的，但我们可以推测它是全球趋势的例证。尽管有这种细微差别，但数据令人沮丧。
根据PitchBook的数据，第三季度，美国的女性创始人和联合创始人筹集了136轮资金，价值4.34亿美元。这比2020年第二季度多了几轮，但美元要少得多。与2019年第三季度相比，它全面下降。更重要的是，正如我们在这篇文章中指出的那样，总体风险投资界表现得非常好。
以下是普华永道(PwC)的一些数据表明了这一点，还有我的老雇主Crunchbase提供的更多数据。重要的是，当风投非常活跃时，女性创始人的表现会更差。这只会使创业市场中的不平等和不公平永久化。
说到这里，还有一些坏消息。根据Crunchbase的数据，Hallo初创公司的联合创始人兼首席执行官小弗恩·霍华德(Vern Howard Jr.)汇编了一些黑人创始人在第三季度的风险投资表现。Hallo是一家初创公司，根据Crunchbase的数据，该公司已经筹集了近200万美元的资金。以下是他开始做的事情：
[W]我们希望为这么多风险资本家的承诺提供坚实的数字，并为我们如何随着时间的推移追踪对黑人创始人的投资建立一个基准。所以我们的团队从Crunchbase上取了一份名单，列出了全球所有的初创公司，总融资金额在50万美元到2000万美元之间，他们在7月1日到10月1日之间进行了一轮融资。这里有1383多家公司，我们团队逐一查看了有多少黑人创始人。
现在，你可以开放资助范围，既包括规模较小的资助活动，也包括规模较大的资助活动，但不管数据边界如何，由此产生的数字-仅占总数的2.2%-是一个耻辱。
OpenDoor-SPAC的交易本周变得更加明朗，因为我们在其财务状况上遇到了麻烦。有些数据是粗略的。有些是好的。但我们现在可以理解其未来的牛市情况，这是有帮助的。
确认宣布已私下提交上市申请，而Root则公开提交上市申请。这里有更多肯定的新闻，这里有对根S-1的深入挖掘。
一定要检查一下YCharts退出私募股权公司的情况。这家初创公司告诉TechCrunch，它今年的年度经常性收入将超过1500万美元。这应该意味着它卖了一大笔钱。
Greycroft筹集了大量新资本，从3.1亿美元的早期支票基金到3.68亿美元的成长期交易基金之间的价差高达6.78亿美元。
我们为什么要关心格雷克罗夫特？就本质而言，我们没有，但值得注意的是，在当前的危机中，风投们仍然能够筹集资金。我们经常谈论创始人在COVID期间是如何设法筹集资金的，这也是风投们必须做的事情。
TechCrunch深入研究了API创业领域，与风投和创始人讨论了这个领域，以及为什么这个领域会在2020年爆炸。
Airbnb的夏天是痛苦的，但它的反弹可能会被证明是传奇。交易所调查了它是如何如此迅速地反弹的。
继续我们对金融科技在世界各地的初创企业今年一直在利用的储蓄和投资热潮的报道，英国罗宾汉(如果你愿意的话)Freetrade告诉交易所，它在9月份的订单量超过了10亿GB。那还不错！
Freetrade最近还推出了其服务的付费版本，因为罗宾汉正在增长的那种按订单流付费的创收方式是不允许跨越大西洋的。
坚持金融科技的世界，Yotta Savings是一家为用户提供储蓄选择的初创公司，将他们的钱存放在这家初创公司可以增加赢得一笔巨额奖金的机会。人们在我耳边谈论这家公司已经有一段时间了，但我一直推迟到现在才写下来，因为我不清楚这种模式是一个噱头，还是真正能吸引客户的东西。
在过去的几周里，Yotta的账户从8000个增加到了3万多个，并达到了1亿美元的存款大关。所以，我想我们现在在乎了。
Coinbase在政治时期对其首席执行官认为与其核心使命无关的任何事情保持中立，这一新战略让每20名员工中就有一人失去了Coinbase。Coinbase作为一家拥有结构性财务支持的营利性公司，正在盈利。
在同一话题上，CAN在空白处提出了一个突出的观点，即“没有政治就是一种政治立场”。正确，而且在这一点上是非常保守的。
更重要的是，Coinbase的首席执行官大声疾呼，他的公司将如何“努力创造一种环境，让每个人都能受到欢迎，并能尽其所能地工作，而不受背景、性取向、种族、性别、年龄等因素的影响。”不管他喜欢与否，这都是一种政治立场，与公司宣称的核心使命无关。这是一场政治斗争赢得的-即平等进入工作场所。
我将为TechCrunch发表的一篇关于此事的风投文章以及一位技术人员关于如何保证你的公司在几乎所有事情上都站在历史错误一边的想法提供一个插头。
这周包装了我们的手提包，Ping Identity买了ShoCard。平安现在是一家上市公司，所以通常情况下，它的交易会在我们的驾驶室之外进行。但在这种情况下，我们关心这一点，因为TechCrunch已经涵盖了ShoCard(2015年：“ShoCard是区块链上的数字身份证”)，也因为这家初创公司从事与密码相关的工作。
看到一家上市公司故意并大声地以真金白银抢购一家区块链初创公司，并不是每天都会发生的事情。如果您想阅读有关交易的更多信息，请单击此处。
包装，这份时事通讯很有趣，感谢您的阅读。这也是一项正在进行的工作。所以，请随时点击并回复，让我知道您想看更多的内容。或者点击回复，给我发一张你宠物的可爱照片。我都不介意。
note_en:
First, a big congrats on making it through the week. If you live in the United States, you just endured one of the wildest news weeks ever. Rapid-fire headlines and nigh-panic have been our lot since last Friday when the president announced he was COVID-19 positive. We’re all very tired. You get points for just surviving.
 Second, I wanted to bring you something uplifting this weekend, as you deserve it. Sadly, that’s not what we’re going to talk about.
  On Friday,  The Exchange covered new data concerning the venture capital results of female founders during the third quarter. The data set was U.S.-focused, but we can presume that it is illustrative of global trends. Regardless of that nuance, the data was depressing.
 In the third quarter, U.S.-based female founders and co-founders raised 136 rounds worth $434 million,  per PitchBook data. That was a handful more rounds than Q2 2020, but far fewer dollars. And it was down across the board compared to Q3 2019. Even more, as we noted in the piece, the aggregate venture capital world did very well.
 Here’s  some PwC data making that point, and  a bit more from my old employer Crunchbase. What matters is that female founders are doing worse when VCs are super active. This will only perpetuate inequalities and inequities in the startup market.
 Speaking of which, here’s some more bad news.  Vern Howard Jr., the co-founder and CEO of  Hallo, a startup that has raised nearly $2 million,  according to Crunchbase, compiled some data on Black founders’ VC performance in Q3. Here’s  what he set out to do:
 [W]e wanted to put hard numbers behind the promises of so many venture capitalists and create a benchmark for how we can track the investment into black founders over time. So our team pulled a list from Crunchbase of all the startups globally with a total funding amount of $500,000 — $20,000,000 and who raised a round between July 1 and October 1. There were over 1383 companies here and our team went through one by one, to see how many Black founders there were.
  Now, you could open up the funding bands to include both smaller and larger funding events, but regardless of the data boundaries, the resulting number — just 2.2% of the total — is a disgrace.
   The OpenDoor-SPAC deal became clearer this week as  we got a scratch at its financials. Some of the data is rough. Some of it is good. But we can now understand the bull case for its future, which helps.
 Affirm announced that it has filed privately to go public, while Root filed publicly to go public. More on the Affirm news  here and a dig into  the Root S-1 here.
 Make sure to check out  YCharts’ exit to a PE firm. The startup told TechCrunch that it will pass $15 million in annual recurring revenue this year. Which should mean that it sold for a pretty penny.
 Greycroft  raised an ocean of new capital, to the tune of $678 million spread between a $310 million fund for early-stage checks, and a $368 million fund targeting growth-stage deals.
 Why do we care about Greycroft? We don’t, per se, but it is worth noting that VCs are still able to raise during the current crisis. We  often talk about how founders are managing to raise during COVID, and this is something that VCs have to do, as well.
 TechCrunch  dug into the API startup space, talking to VCs and founders alike about the space and why it’s blowing up in 2020.
 Airbnb’s summer was painful, but its rebound may prove legendary. The Exchange  examined how it managed to bounce back so quickly.
   Continuing our coverage of  the savings and investing boom that fintech startups  around the world have been riding this year,  Freetrade, a British Robinhood if you will, told The Exchange that it crossed £1 billion in September order volume. That’s not bad!
 Freetrade also recently launched a paid version of its service, as the payment-for-order-flow method of generating revenue  that Robinhood is growing on the back of is not allowed across the pond.
 Sticking to the fintech world,  Yotta Savings is a startup that provides a savings option to its users, with the added chance of winning a big monetary prize for having stored their money with the startup. Folks have been whispering in my ear about the company for a bit, but I’ve held off writing about it until now as it was not clear to me if the model was merely a gimmick, or something that would actually attract customers.
 Well, Yotta grew from 8,000 accounts to more than 30,000 in the past few weeks and has reached the $100 million deposit mark. So, I guess we now care.
 Coinbase  lost one in 20 employees to its new strategy of standing neutral during political times on anything that its CEO deems as unrelated to its core mission, which, as a for-profit company with tectonic financial backing, is making money.
 On the same topic,  Can from  The Margins  made a salient point that “no politics is a political stance.” Correct, and it is a very conservative one at that.
 Even more, Coinbase’s CEO  made noise about how his company will “work to create an environment where everyone is welcome and can do their best work, regardless of background, sexual orientation, race, gender, age, etc.” Whether he likes it or not, this is a political stance, and one that has nothing to do with the company’s stated core mission. And a political fight earned it — namely, equal access to the workplace.
 I’ll toss in a plug for  this piece on the matter from a VC that TechCrunch published, and  these thoughts from a tech denizen on how to guarantee that your company lands on the wrong side of history on essentially everything.
 Wrapping our grab-bag this week,  Ping Identity bought ShoCard. Ping is now a public company, so normally its deals would land outside our wheelhouse. But we care in this case because TechCrunch has covered ShoCard (2015: “ ShoCard Is A Digital Identity Card On The Blockchain”), and because the startup does crypto-related work.
 Seeing a public company snap up a blockchain startup for real money, on purpose and out loud, doesn’t happen every day. More  here if you want to read about the deal.
  Wrapping, this newsletter is a lot of fun and I appreciate your reading it. It is, also, a work in progress. So feel free to hit respond to it and let me know what you want to see more of. Or hit respond and send me a cute pic of your pet. Either is fine by me.

note:
(CNN)你以前听过这句话--给无家可归的人钱不是帮助他们的最好方式，因为钱可能会被挥霍掉，或者花在有害的习惯上。但加拿大的一项新研究提出了一个强有力的相反理由。
这项名为“新叶计划”的研究是由社会变革基金会发起的，该基金会总部设在温哥华，是一家慈善组织，与不列颠哥伦比亚省大学合作。
研究人员向50名最近无家可归的人一次性支付了7500加元(近5700美元)。他们对现金接受者的寿命进行了超过12-18个月的跟踪调查，并将他们的结果与没有收到付款的对照组的结果进行了比较。
将于明年接受同行审查的初步调查结果显示，平均而言，那些获得现金的人能够更快地找到稳定的住房。相比之下，那些没有收到现金的人在获得更多永久住房方面落后了大约12个月。
接受现金的人能够获得他们生活更快所需的食物。近70%的人在一个月后做到了这一点，并在全年保持了更大的粮食安全。
受助者在食物、衣服和房租上的花费更多，而在酒类、香烟或药物等商品上的支出减少了39%。
无家可归的人口继续增长，我们一直在采用同样的旧方法，社会变革基金会的首席执行官兼联合创始人克莱尔·威廉姆斯(Claire Williams)说。
威廉姆斯补充说，我们真的认为，以社会变革的名义开始测试有意义的冒险行为是很重要的。
这项随机对照试验的115名参与者年龄在19岁到64岁之间，他们无家可归的平均时间为6个月。参与者接受了心理健康挑战和药物滥用的低风险筛查。
该倡议的资金来自加拿大联邦政府的赠款，以及该国的捐赠者和基金会。
威廉姆斯告诉CNN，最令人震惊的一件事是，大多数收到现金的人立刻就知道他们想要用这笔钱做什么，这与人们的刻板印象背道而驰。
例如，她解释说，一些接受现金的人知道他们想用这笔钱搬进住房，或者投资于交通--买辆自行车，或者把车开到修理店，以便保住自己的工作。其他人想买电脑。他们中的一些人想要开始自己的小生意。
威廉姆斯说：人们非常清楚自己需要什么，但我们往往不会给他们提供真正赋予他们选择和尊严的干预或服务，让他们能够按照自己的方式前进。
威廉姆斯说，通常情况下，直接现金转移并不是解决无家可归问题的灵丹妙药，该计划关注的是无家可归人口中功能较高的一部分，但她认为，研究表明，为最近无家可归的人提供有意义的支持会降低他们在无家可归经历中扎根的可能性。
接受现金注射的人甚至可以留出一些钱-在12个月内平均约为1000加元。
根据威廉姆斯的说法，该项目提供的资金也产生了涓滴效应，人们在孩子的福祉和需求上投入了更多资金，同时也帮助了家庭成员。
威廉姆斯解释说，有一些隐藏的影响，我们只是没有预料到，也没有必要量化，但现在我们重新看到，这对人们的生活产生了指数级的影响。
根据这项研究，减少50名接受现金的研究参与者在避难所度过的夜晚数，每人每年节省约8100加元，或对所有50名参与者来说，一年节省约40.5万加元。
威廉姆斯说，有一种常见的误解，认为无所事事的代价是免费的或便宜的，但绝对不是。
对于美国各地的许多人来说，无家可归的风险迫在眉睫，因为人们正在应对冠状病毒大流行带来的失业和经济不确定性。
美国非营利组织“全国结束无家可归联盟”(National Alliance To End Homelless)副总裁史蒂夫·伯格(Steve Berg)告诉CNN，我们从很多地方的无家可归者那里听说，以前从未无家可归过的人纷纷来到收容所，他们不知道该怎么办。
对他们来说，这是一种全新的体验，他们做梦也没想到，当他们谈论无家可归的人时，他们会谈论自己。没有参与“新叶计划”研究的伯格说。
这个问题的创新解决方案现在更加紧迫。伯格认为，这项研究证实了对许多无家可归的人来说是正确的：金钱可以解决这个问题。
当然也有无家可归的人有更深、更严重的问题，伯格解释说，但对许多人来说，这只是一个问题--他们花光了钱，失去了工作，陷入困境，无家可归。一旦他们无家可归，就很难攒够钱找地方住。
伯格补充说，如果人们预先拿到了钱，他们可以信赖自己来解决这个问题。
note_en:
(CNN)You've heard this refrain before -- giving money to homeless people is not the best way to help them because it might be squandered, or spent on harmful habits. But a new Canadian study makes a powerful case to the contrary.
 The study, dubbed " The New Leaf Project," is an initiative of Foundations for Social Change, a charitable organization based in Vancouver, in partnership with the University of British Columbia.
 Researchers gave 50 recently homeless people a lump sum of 7,500 Canadian dollars (nearly $5,700). They followed the cash recipients' life over 12-18 months and compared their outcomes to that of a control group who didn't receive the payment.
   The preliminary  findings, which will be peer-reviewed next year, show that those who received cash were able to find stable housing faster, on average. By comparison, those who didn't receive cash lagged about 12 months behind in securing more permanent housing .
 People who received cash were able to access the food they needed to live  faster. Nearly 70% did after one month, and maintained greater food security throughout the year.
  The recipients spent more on food, clothing and rent, while there was a 39% decrease in spending on goods like alcohol, cigarettes or drugs.
 "The homeless population continues to grow, and we keep applying the same old approaches," said Claire Williams, the CEO and co-founder of Foundations for Social Change.
 "We really think it's important to start testing meaningful risk-taking in the name of social change," WIlliams added.
 The 115 participants in the randomized controlled trial were between the ages of 19 and 64, and they had been homeless for an average of 6 months. Participants were screened for a low risk of mental health challenges and substance abuse.
 Funding for the initiative came from a grant from the Canadian federal government, and from donors and foundations in the country.
  "One of the things that was most striking is that most people who received the cash knew immediately what they wanted to do with that money, and that just flies in the face of stereotypes," Williams told CNN.
 For example, she explained some cash recipients knew they wanted to use the money to move into housing, or invest in transportation -- getting a bike, or taking their cars to the repair shop to be able to keep their jobs. Others wanted to purchase computers. A number of them wanted to start their own small businesses.
 "People very much know what they need, but we often don't equip them with the intervention or the services that really empowers them with choice and dignity to move forward on their own terms," Williams said.
  Direct cash transfers are not "a silver bullet for homelessness in general," and the program focused on "a higher functioning subset of the homeless population," Williams said, but she believes the research shows that providing meaningful support to folks who have recently become homeless decreases the likelihood they will become entrenched in the experience.
 People who received the cash infusions were even able to set some money aside -- about 1,000 Canadian dollars on average through 12 months.
 The money provided by the program also had "trickle down impacts," according to Williams, with people investing more in their children's well-being and needs, as well as helping out family members.
 "There are these hidden impacts that we just don't anticipate and aren't necessarily quantifying, but now we're seeing that this is having an exponential effect on people's lives," Williams explained.
  According to the research, reducing the number of nights spent in shelters by the 50 study participants who received cash saved approximately 8,100 Canadian dollars per person per year, or about 405,000 Canadian dollars over one year for all 50 participants.
 "There's a common misconception that the cost of doing nothing is free or cheap and it absolutely is not," Williams said.
  The risk of homelessness looms large for many across the United States, as people deal with job losses and economic uncertainty brought about by the coronavirus pandemic.
 "We're hearing that from homeless providers in a lot of places, people who have never been homeless before are coming into shelters and have no idea what to do," Steve Berg, a vice president with the  National Alliance to End Homelessness, an American nonprofit organization, told CNN.
 "It's a brand new experience for them, and they never dreamed that when they'd be talking about homeless people they'd be talking about themselves," said Berg, who was not involved in The New Leaf Project study.
 Innovative solutions to the issue are even more pressing right now. Berg thinks the research confirms what is true for many people experiencing homelessness: money can solve it.
   "There are certainly people who are homeless who have deeper, more severe problems," Berg explained, "but for many people, it's simply a matter of -- they ran out of money, lost a job, fell on hard times, became homeless. Once they're homeless, it's very difficult to get enough money saved up in order to find a place to live."
 "People can be relied on, if they get the money upfront, to take care of the problem themselves," Berg added.

据报道，谷歌有限责任公司(Google LLC)正计划将其开源Kative项目的直接控制权让给一个由五个席位组成的指导委员会，该委员会将制定规则，防止任何一个组织拥有超过两个席位。
据科技新闻网站The Protocol今天的一篇报道，该计划旨在平息外界对谷歌秘密计划保持对其开发的关键开源项目控制权的批评。Kative是一个开源项目，最先由谷歌开发，提供在Kubernetes之上部署、运行和管理无服务器、云原生应用的组件。Kubernetes是一个容器管理平台，也是由谷歌打造的，于2015年开源。
无服务器应用程序可自动扩展和缩小以响应事件，无需手动增加额外的计算资源，从而提高了开发人员的工作效率并降低了运营成本。Kative是为公司提供一种方式来利用无服务器模式，容器应用程序可以在任何类型的计算平台上运行。
报道称，谷歌正计划对Kative的治理结构做出一些重大改变。该委员会的席位现在将由个人而不是特定的公司持有，今年晚些时候将举行选举，选出两名新成员。此外，报道称，谷歌正在考虑最终将该委员会扩大到七名成员，以此作为纳入Kative用户社区代表的一种方式。
就在几个月前，谷歌违背了将另一个项目Istio的控制权移交给云本地计算基金会(Cloud Native Computing Foundation)的承诺，激怒了开源软件界的一些成员。云本地计算基金会是Linux基金会的一个项目，成立于2015年，目的是帮助推进容器技术。
今年7月，谷歌表示，它将创建一个名为Open Usage Commons的中立组织来管理其商标政策，而不是将Istio转移到CNCF，而控制将由该项目的指导委员会保持。
这一决定令谷歌的许多合作伙伴感到不安，其中最引人注目的是IBM公司，该公司也为Istio的发展做出了巨大贡献。一些人表示，此举甚至可能是谷歌保持对Istio未来方向控制的秘密方式。批评人士说，问题在于谷歌控制了Istio指导委员会中有投票权的多数席位，这让开源支持者感到不安，他们认为Kubernetes这样的项目的中立性是他们成功的主要原因之一。
谷歌终于在8月份采取行动平息了这些批评，宣布将改变Istio的治理结构，以防止任何一家公司获得控制权。

note_en:
Google LLC is reportedly planning to relinquish direct control over its open-source Knative project to a five-seat steering committee that will have rules to prevent any single organization from having more than two seats.
 The plan is designed to stymie criticism that Google is secretly planning to retain control over key open-source projects it has developed, according to a  report today on the tech news website The Protocol.  Knative is an open-source project first developed by Google that provides components for deploying, running and managing serverless, cloud-native applications on top of Kubernetes, a container management platform that was also built by Google and open-sourced in 2015.
 Serverless apps automatically scale up and down in response to events without the need to spin up extra computing resources manually, leading to increased developer productivity and reduced operational costs. Knative was built to give companies a way to take advantage of the serverless model with container apps that can run on any kind of computing platform.
 Google is planning to make some major changes to Knative’s governance structure, according to the report. Seats on the committee will now be held by individuals rather than specific companies, and elections will be held later this year to select two new members. In addition, the report said, Google is considering eventually expanding the committee to seven members as a way to include representatives from Knative’s user community.
 The plan comes just a few months after Google angered some members of the open-source software community when it  reneged on a promise to hand over control of another project,  Istio, to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation, a Linux Foundation project that was founded in 2015 to help advance container technology.
 In July Google said that it instead of transferring Istio to the CNCF, it would create a neutral organization called Open Usage Commons to manage its trademark policies, while control would be maintained by the project’s steering committee.
 That decision  upset many of Google’s partners, most notably IBM Corp., which has also contributed greatly to the development of Istio. Some said the move could even be a secret way for Google to retain control over the future direction of Istio. The problem, critics said, was that Google controlled a voting majority of the seats on Istio’s steering committee, something that unnerves open-source proponents who believe the neutrality of projects like Kubernetes is one of the main reasons for their success.
 Google finally moved to calm those critics in August, announcing it would  change Istio’s governance structure to prevent any one company from taking control.
